author: dampedspring
text:
Fantastic

Try the crystal ball game!
"You can't convince anyone to change their mind"
92 year old mother day trades story.
Risk is a cost
Don't take risk that others can diversify away.
How can I become a billionaire is the wrong question
Save and keep your savings at every age

The open question related to all of this is still why people have become more  extreme risk seekers.  Vic and I talked about that in person recently and I think we concluded the shift is true and also somewhat psychology pathological.

My favorite part is for someone to run at  15 vol risk they don't need to beat cash by much.  It can be had with unlevered SPX but to go to 30 vol you need to get paid 9%-2.25% or 675bp outperformance vs SPX just to "breakeven" on a risk adjusted basis. To go to 50% risk which most likely can only be done with some leverage (costly and risks
Margin calls) and lot of concentration in single idiosyncratic assets like stocks and crypto you need to make 25%-2.25% or 22.5% MORE than the SPX.  It's a huge hurdle rate in which leveraged beta providers who pick assets and concentrate brag about 500bp of SPX outperformance for these levels of risk.  The outperformance needs to be way way higher to justify these strategies

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
That’s a misconception:

$SIVE is the laser supplier for next gen architectures, not just CPO scale up. 

Pluggable, scale out CPO, scale up CPO, NPO, etc. 

- Sivers and $JBL went god-mode and developed 1.6T optical transceivers with CW lasers.

Effectively designing around the EML bottlenecks, and even made things more power efficient. 

From quotes from Jabil management they created a “relatively dramatic moat”.

So for the next gen of 1.6T pluggable transceivers, Sivers seems immediately used.

Markets missed this nuance too: after Jabil’s announcement, other pluggable players reached out, and SIVE is working with them now (prob codevelopment, qualification stage).

There’s not too many players that build optical transceivers that aren’t vertically integrated like Lumentum (think: Innolight/eoptolink, and maybe others).

So these are active developments, just not public material yet and could be a new press release anytime.

- for CPO scale out which happens H2 2026 onward like $POET, $SIVE is a laser supplier for those players.

- scale up CPO is h2 2027, from Ayar and Nvidia’a NVLink CPO ecosystem and that’s the main volume ramp across optical players.

But this is where $SIVE looks like they mog every other player from supply chain mapping.

Since they’ve likely been working + designed in with $MRVL Celestial, Lightmatter, Lightelligence and the others since they were small way back when.

On top of things like this:

For foundries like $GFS, $SIVE is the reference laser.

Not even including massive companies like O-Net building ELS with $SIVE, likely for the Asian hyperscalers supply chains.

This is why $SIVE is by far my favorite laser chokepoint long.

Feels like they’re everywhere in new optical architectures starting beginning of 2027.

Mapping like

- JP Morgan fireside conference for Jabil timelines + Quotes around 1.6T LRO

- Sivers 2025 annual around $POET various press releases with $GFS and others.

- Old investor customers decks mapped with different investment rounds of cpo startups at the time

All merged together into a thesis.

@eldan_0123 @CreatorNim Don’t think so. $JBL + $SIVE was known from OFC conference in person. 

But it took an official PR for markets to know.

If there any release over the more higher confidence mapping to $NVDA cpo ecosystem, I’d expect it to rerate a lot more

@jeongpark0509 No price target, but I like to keep in mind:

$LITE went from 2.88B MC in 2024, to $67B MC in 2026.

Just took 2 years and $SIVE is around the same starting point as Lumentum today.

author: aleabitoreddit
text:
I've been getting a lot of questions about OE Solutions (138080) recently. 

Here's my research/thoughts so far on it: 

They're a small Korean optical transceiver company, similar to $AAOI. 

And they've become one the few EML players in the world (eg. $COHR, $LITE, Mitsubishi, Source, Sumitomo), with scarce 100G EML laser capacity for 800G/1.6T. 

OE appears to be trying to make the full transceiver, not just the EML. They also have finished ELSFP CPO products, with UHP  CW lasers, which is sampling Q3. 

Likewise, OE also seems to be building out the full ELSFP, rather than selling CW laser dies, so that's more market share. 

So you can think of it as Korean AAOI but EML instead of CW, and less capacity/qualifications. And playing catch-up to the rest of the world. 

However in terms of timelines:

1. 23dBm cooled ELSFP samples start in Q3 2026
2. Sales base for 800G, and 2027 1.6T "full force"
3. Late H2 2027, H1 2028 onward probably their ELSFP enters the volume production. 

And it seems they're working on 200G EML capability from their investor snipper 2025 OE IR snippet referenced “100GBaud EML / 200G PAM". This seems promising given their IP/demos.

ELS has just been unveiled recently, sampling starts Q3. 800G/1.6T Optical transceivers are also likely 2027.

So this is basically Korea's sovereign photonics player, playing catchup to $LITE, $AAOI, and the bigger players.

(Disclosure: I have positions in OE Solutions (138080). This is fundamental research for informational purposes, not financial advice).

As for OE solution valuations: 

1.  Doesn't seem like there's confirmed customers yet for these growth verticals. 
2. Probably not many people understood what they're building toward yet. 
3. Yields data kinda uncertain

1. Customers: We're actually in a major EML/CW laser shortage, so any independent capacity will be sought after. I personally don't think they'll have a hard time finding customers here. I would assume anything they make might get bought out and would get extra support from Korea. 

2. Institutional support: Probably not much since it doesn't meet threshold for many US institution given MC size + KR listing. I also don't think many people understood what they're building yet. 

3. Yields/Capacity: OE's disclosed wafer/module utilization is low (i remember was around 31% underutilized off the top of my head), so there's enough material revenue they can generate before they need to spend on capex. 

I'm also not sure about EML/CW and other yields. Probably need to go ask the company. 

Is this some random crap co? No. It's been doing optical transceivers stuff for more than 20Y, has R&D in the U.S. and Netherlands. 

But their entire AI growth vertical seems to happen next year, and hinges mainly around capacity/yields.

And I personally think the EML/CW tech is probably worth a lot more than their current MC, if it were an acquisition target. 

Especially from  a larger player that wanted to vertically integrate EML for pluggables and CW lasers for CPO. 

Markets are probably waiting on more certainty around qualifications after Q3 sampling or earnings projections announcements.  

TLDR on thoughts:

Some of my friends discussed this last year, was probably way too early. Saw it got many comments 2 months ago, still too early. 

I still think now is early, but later in Q3-Q4 might be more interesting. I personally think it's a lot higher risk than a major CPO player like $SIVE, that's embedded in Ayar, $JBL, $GFS, and many other hyperscaler suppliers. Which also has Win Semi and others de-risking volume ramp. 

As OE Solutions looks like a new player trying to build out an $AAOI for the optical transceivers but sovereign EML and CW laser production for CPO products. 

And there's a lot of answered questions around customers + volume ramp, which presents material risk. 

But if you believe Korea can build out an 800G/1.6T transceiver EML supply chain and ELSFP with CW lasers. 

With OE Solutions, it might be worth taking a look into. Still researching the company tho, just initial thoughts.

@MichaelIsOnFire Thanks! Yeah I'm not a fan of paywalling/selling research to institutions. 

Just prefer to share my thoughts for free to people who want to spend the time and read it.

@Beyucu88 光迅科技 (https://t.co/YLWS3Dq98d) and 东山精密 (https://t.co/NBKJVrwXqu)

Korea is far behind with lasers. Maybe like 1 1/2 years if I had to guess. 

Which is why OE Solutions is the very first/only to produce 100G EML in Korea AFAIK. 

US ($LITE, $COHR) / Japan (Sumitomo, etc). dominates EML lasers. China has now kinda caught up. 

US/Sweden/Japan dominates CW DFB IP. 

But people are rightly saying a few in the world can do this, and the 100G EML IP/process is valuable.

Nittobo (3110) is one of the most frustrating Japanese companies I've seen. They hold an monopoly in glass fiber cloth, like ~90%+ market share.

But refuse to price hike T-Glass ASP, beyond something small like 20-30%.

Could easy triple company valuations if they followed $SNDK + NAND but just due to Japanese culture, they won't do it.

We're in a massive EML bottleneck right now and CW lasers are getting bottlenecked now too. 

To my knowledge, $COHR is buying EMLs off $LITE because they can't make enough. 

$LITE is majority allocated to EML, so they can't make enough CW lasers. 

So they're buying CW lasers off competitors from their ER transcript, (probably Sumitomo, Furukawa and others), which likely feeds into $NVDA contracts. 

Then $AMD and your hyperscalers need capacity too but we're already in a shortage. 

Any capacity that comes online would likely be bought, since looks like we're in a shortage for next few years (lumentum already sold out into 2028).  

Not quite the same as your sk hynix/samsung/micron memory dynamic but there's still a massive moat with EML with probably only single digit amounts of players able to do this.

author: dgt10011
text:
In a world awash in performative attention, where charlatanry is celebrated and wealth is mistaken for character, remember that fatherhood remains one of life’s highest callings: a lifelong commitment to honor, duty, and integrity

The moral anchor of Family

Happy Father’s Day!

I asked my son today, “What do you think it means to be a father?”

He said it must be hard work

I asked why

He relied: “sometimes you have to do things with me that you don’t really want to, so that must be hard when you want to be alone”

Broke my heart a bit

I’m going to do better

author: DCinvestor
text:
i don't have much to new add to the current discussions around the EF, so i'll just reiterate what i've said in the past:

1) R&D activities should be spun out of the EF and managed separately via something resembling an industry consortium process. having this function sit alongside ecosystem dev has created several instances of conflicts of interest at best and suboptimal protocol progress misaligned with market demands at worst
2) we increasingly have new organizations (like DATs) which hold a lot of ETH who could catalyze a new approach to a protocol development along these lines
3) any remaining ecosystem dev activities at the EF should ideally be dramatically reduced tightly scoped and focused on areas requiring coordination between market participants where it would not exist otherwise (i.e., coordination failures)
4) proposals for in-protocol funding from issuance or fees should be rejected as they will compromise neutrality and turn Ethereum into "just another blockchain"

that the current debates have been started again via staff departures isn't a surprise. nor is discussion of funding crises in a bear market

there are real issues which require long-term resolution. maybe this time that will happen. if not, the decentralized protocol remains on solid ground imo. frankly, everything else could mostly disappear and most people wouldn't care

i will say that historically, when these discussions reach fever pitch, it has often been the best time to buy ETH as it is often near the bear market bottom

author: udiWertheimer
text:
@AlgodTrading IF they dramatically reduce subsidies, they dramatically reduce spend on new hardware

if they dramatically reduce spend on new hardware, hardware prices go down, and you can buy much more powerful gpus then.

it’s probably a when, yeah. although it could conceivably be a while!

i think we will eventually see very capable consumer hardware with very fair prices. again, it’s a matter of when. but i think my point is that it’s likely to roughly coincide with the ending of subsidies. so economically it makes sense to take advantage of subsidies today, and own inference when they stop

the idea that some people are promoting that consumer hardware will sustainably go up in price when subsidies end and therefore you should buy inference now is imo incorrect

@AlgodTrading in crypto we’ve seen this in 2020-ish, the proliferation of industrial mining caused a real spike in consumer gpu prices, but as soon as mining operations stopped leveraging up, consumer prices dropped

AI is on a much larger scale of course but the concept is similar

the demand comes from anthropic and openai having infinite access to capital, which they use to subsidize (run training and inference at a loss)

once they can no longer do that for whatever reason, demand necessarily shrinks substantially. they’re pouring like $100B into the market every quarter. like you said this can’t last forever

they’re essentially front-loading all the demand now instead of letting it show up later when the models are more capable, to increase their chances of winning the race. that’s the essence of the vc capital model

so the demand you’re seeing now is already dislocated. once the models become materially more capable, demand probably goes down, because the race will be over

i’m not disagreeing with you. what i’m saying is right now openai and anthropic are front-running future gpu demand. and they’re insensitive to price because they currently have infinite access to capital and they want to get to the finish line first

as we both seem to agree, this regime will eventually end. their access to capital will shrink and their willingness to spend all of it at any price will be over. once that happens gpu prices go down

of course as prices go down demand will show up from other businesses and consumers. but it’ll not be enough to fully replace the current demand from anthropic and openai because they’re already compressing years of future gpu demand into each quarter

@aizorr0 @AlgodTrading i really am agreeing with that, i’m just saying this is already priced into the current gpu demand from anthropic and openai. jevons doesn’t account for all of the capital in the world agreeing to front-load all of the demand in the beginning of the process

author: udiWertheimer
text:

author: stacy_muur
text:
One of Ethereum's biggest upgrades is now in the final testing phase.

Mainnet is loosely targeted for the second half of 2026, and one of the EF devs is already calling it the biggest fork since the Merge.

These are some of the planned upgrades:

→ ePBS (EIP-7732): block-building gets baked into Ethereum itself
→ Block-level access lists (EIP-7928): blocks start running in parallel
→ Gas repricing: compute gets cheaper, state growth gets pricier
→ Gas limit: increased over time from ~60M toward 200M

IMO, the most significant change is ePBS.

Right now, close to 90% of blocks are built by outside builders and passed through offchain relays like MEV-Boost, which is critical infrastructure that Ethereum doesn't control.

ePBS builds that step directly into Ethereum, which cuts out the relay middleman and the ordering/censorship risk that comes with it.

Do you think this will put ETH into the spotlight again?

author: Swedish_uranium
text:
Deal or no deal? Once again, that's the market's question.

Here are my Sunday thoughts on #uranium, #gold, #silver, #copper, #fluorspar and #potash.

A week ago, investors were debating whether a US-Iran agreement would stabilize the region and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Today, the picture is far less clear. Iran has once again announced the closure of Hormuz, reminding markets that geopolitics can overwhelm diplomacy overnight.

I still believe some form of agreement is the most likely outcome. Iran needs oil revenues. The US wants lower energy prices, lower inflation, and eventually lower interest rates. The incentives remain powerful. But the road to a lasting deal may be far more complicated than many expected.

What continues to impress me most is uranium. Despite one of the most significant geopolitical disruptions in years, the long-term uranium price continues to trend higher. Utilities still face the same challenge: growing reactor demand and limited future mine supply.

Gold and silver have pulled back, but if tensions ease and oil prices move lower, I expect markets to begin pricing in future rate cuts again. A weaker USD, improving liquidity, and rising sovereign debt levels would be supportive for both metals.

Copper remains one of my highest-conviction long-term themes. AI infrastructure, data centers, electrification, grid expansion, and EV adoption all require enormous amounts of copper, while major discoveries remain scarce and permitting timelines continue to lengthen.

One mineral that deserves far more attention is fluorspar. It is essential for uranium conversion, steelmaking, aluminum production, semiconductors, batteries, and numerous chemical processes. As governments push to secure domestic critical mineral supply chains, fluorspar projects in stable jurisdictions could become increasingly strategic.

And it's not just energy security that matters. It's food security too. Disruptions in energy markets often ripple through fertilizer markets. Higher energy and transportation costs ultimately affect agricultural production. That's one reason I remain bullish on potash.

The world needs more energy. More electricity. More infrastructure. More critical minerals. And more food.

That's why I remain extremely bullish on uranium, copper, gold, silver, fluorspar, and potash over the coming years.

Uranium > $300, gold > $10,000, silver > $200, and substantially higher copper prices would not surprise me at all.

The long-term story remains intact.

Just my thoughts. Please do your own DD.

Follow me for regular market updates and visit my profile to see my top picks.

author: Swedish_uranium
text:
With #uranium approaching a structural supply crunch, #silver deficits persisting, #copper demand accelerating, and critical minerals becoming a strategic priority, here's where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward in the market.

My top picks across uranium, silver, copper, potash, fluorspar, and graphene 👇

These are not necessarily the safest investments. They are simply the companies where I currently see the most attractive risk/reward.

☢️ URANIUM

Producers & Developers
$CCJ – The blue-chip uranium name.
$KAP – Lowest-cost producer in the world.
$DNN – Fully permitted and advancing Wheeler River.
$NXE – One of the world's largest undeveloped uranium projects.
$UUUU – This week they received a conditional US government loan commitment of up to $725 million to expand domestic rare earth processing and strengthen critical mineral supply chains. Exposure to uranium, rare earths, and growing US strategic support.
$EU – Current U.S. production.
$UEC – Unhedged and ramping up production.
Explorers
$SYH – Drilling near Wheeler River and one of the best prospect generators in the Athabasca Basin.
$M.CN – Potentially the largest uranium deposit in the U.S. based on historic drilling and recent radiometric results. 655 Mlbs endowment in the area. Now included in URNJ, drill program about to start.
$NOBL – Recently published a maiden resource at Duck Creek with significant expansion potential.
$FUU – The team behind four discoveries. If JR and Tetra connect, this could become a monster.

🥈 SILVER
I own three established producers with great margins at current silver prices:
$HL
$AG
$PAAS
I also own these three explorers:
$HYMC – Drilling one of the largest precious metals systems in the world.
$GRSL.V – Recently reported a standout drill intercept.
$JTWO – My wildcard. Bonanza-grade silver samples suggest a much larger high-grade system.

🔌 COPPER
$CGNT – My primary copper exposure. A massive deposit with significant leverage to higher copper prices.

🌾 POTASH
$KCLI – Massive exploration target in Utah's Paradox Basin with district-scale potential.

💎 FLUORSPAR
$CTV.V – CleanTech Vanadium is moving from planning to execution. The company recently launched a major drill program at its Campbell-Crotser fluorspar project in Kentucky, targeting material for an initial underground mine plan and a planned 500 tpd processing operation.

🌐 GRAPHENE 
$HGRAF – Still my largest single holding. A unique graphene technology platform with potential applications across multiple industries.

Disclosure: I have paid partnerships with Myriad Uranium, Skyharbour Resources, J2 Metals, Noble Plains Uranium, American Critical Minerals, F3 Uranium and CleanTech Vanadium.

Not financial advice. Please do your own due diligence.

author: PeterDiamandis
PeterDiamandis
thedefiedge
text:
Price barely moved this week, butnthe structure underneath it moved a lot.

Onchain execution passed the CEX, the Fed ditched forward guidance, and that's two of seven.

7 reads worth your time this week:

Onchain execution > CEX execution.

For 1k ETH orders, onchain pricing is now 20 bps tighter than the CEX. The thing making it possible is propAMMs, and they've finally landed on Ethereum.

If you want to understand the primitive, start here.

Moat in crypto

Best businesses position themselves “in the money flow”.

Railroads didn't profit on locomotives. They profited on every ton of grain that crossed the track. Visa, Jane Street, Google, AWS, all the same move.

Here's a framework to identify high-potential crypto projects.

A new era of Fed policy

In his debut policy announcement, the new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh held the rates steady and completely abandoned the Fed's traditional "forward guidance".

Here's a summary of his statement.

Data business is flipping

For all of financial data history, the buyer was a person at a desk. Agents break that. They query, pay per call, and move on, billions of times over.

This piece maps who's building for the new world.

Framework for Neobanks

Winners in crypto neobank race will be hyper-specializers that solves financial pain points that traditional banks cannot solve.

This report divides neobanks into five groups, from full-stack issuers to remittance-first cards.

Laws are holding you back

Companies stay private for a decade now. By IPO, all the upside is gone. And accredited investor laws make it illegal for regular people to buy in early.

Brian is calling for updating the accredited investor laws.

Analyzing Vitalik's stablecoin proposal

Underneath it's a covered call, continuously rolled to stay stable.

The Rysk founder's diagnosis: it dies on demand. You need a standing supply of people wanting levered long ETH in this form, forever.

If you like reads like these, you'll love the free weekly newsletter.

Every Thursday: new strategies, narratives, and the latest in DeFi. No fluff or b.s.

https://t.co/stddYOY4lr

author: Tyler_Neville_
text:
Genuine question for those who have kids heading into overpriced colleges for the next 4 years- How do you justify that cost when AI is scaling like this?

Are universities adjusting for this? To me they feel likes bureaucratic animals where most can’t adjust to the reality fast enough.

My school at the time was $40k a year. It’s now $90k a year.  The value proposition was way higher when you didn’t have AI and you could learn from an expert profession.  The cost of that expertise is dropping exponentially but the cost of the education is rising exponentially- something needs to break.
